extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is an industrial robot used for machining operations. Its architecture comprises multiple articulated joints whose internal joint friction changes with operating conditions, loads, and environmental factors. The robot operates in a high‑precision manufacturing domain where real‑time control and dynamic adaptation of friction models are required.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides (1) real‑time identification of internal joint friction by ingesting measured sensor data, (2) on‑line compensation of the identified friction through the robot’s control system, (3) automatic updating or re‑training of physics‑based (white‑box) and data‑driven (black‑box, neural‑network) friction models as new data arrive, (4) early‑fault detection and predictive‑maintenance alerts when anomalous friction patterns are observed, (5) offline simulation experiments that use the twin to explore alternative machining parameters and detect critical subprocesses before production, and (6) a centralised digital‑shadow data repository that can be accessed by downstream applications for tasks such as CO₂‑optimisation or product‑quality prediction.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on an Asset Administration Shell (AAS) executor that receives measured, simulated and reference values and writes them into a digital shadow stored in the Thingsboard IoT platform. The twin incorporates both physics‑based (white‑box) friction models and data‑driven (black‑box) neural‑network models, which are updated automatically. Communication follows standard IoT protocols, and the control loop integrates the twin‑derived compensation commands. A web‑based dashboard visualises the friction estimates and health indicators.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The digital shadow receives an automated, continuous one‑way data stream from the robot; processing components consume new sensor measurements immediately, enabling real‑time identification of joint friction during runtime. The system is designed for real‑time operation, i.e., the latency between physical measurement and update of the shadow must be bounded by the control‑loop requirements, although exact frequencies or latency numbers are not specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system is orchestrated as an automated pipeline that builds the digital shadow from an Asset Administration Shell (AAS) and live robot data. A processing component consumes incoming data traces, runs executable simulation models (derived from SysML structural models and Simulink behavioral models) to compute friction parameters, and produces updated data traces that are stored in a ThingsBoard IoT platform. Communication between components uses MQTT, and the resulting information is visualised on a dashboard. Traceability is maintained via origins‑association links between data traces, processing components, and the models that generated them, enabling coordinated updates and continuous refinement of the twin.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems relies on standard Industry 4.0 protocols and platforms: MQTT is used as the messaging protocol to connect IoT sensors and devices to the digital shadow; ThingsBoard provides scalable IoT data storage and visualization; the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) serves as the interoperability façade for the twin; and model exchange between SysML structural models and Simulink behavioral models is achieved via the Functional Mock‑up Interface (FMI). These mechanisms allow the twin to interoperate with other enterprise systems, additional Digital Twins, and cloud‑based services.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin combines physics‑based (white‑box) simulation models, e.g., Simulink dynamic simulations, with data‑driven (black‑box) models such as machine‑learning or neural‑network models. Measured data traces captured from the physical system are stored as “measured” values; simulated values are generated by the simulation models; reference values are provided for validation. A Processing component consumes input data traces, executes the calculation or simulation models, and produces output data that become part of the digital shadow. The “originates‑from” association enables full traceability from each data trace back to the processing component, the input traces, and the specific models used. All data and model artefacts are kept in the digital shadow, illustrated as being stored in a Thingsboard‑based repository, and serve as the central data memory for the twin.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is required to reflect variations of friction due to operating conditions, load, temperature, wear and payload changes. The twin continuously updates model parameters or retrains the data‑driven models when new measurement data become available, thereby maintaining accuracy over the robot’s life cycle. Validation is performed by comparing measured data traces with simulated outputs and reference values stored in the digital shadow. Traceability of data traces to the originating processing component and the applied models supports verification of model transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models). Sources of uncertainty are the changing friction behavior and sensor noise; they are mitigated by the dynamic adaptation loop and by maintaining full provenance of data and model artefacts.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The robot’s internal sensors (measuring input current, motor angular velocity and first‑axis position) send their readings to a preprocessing component, which applies a low‑pass filter to the current signal, formats the data as JSON and publishes it via an MQTT broker using hierarchical MQTT topics. The MQTT broker forwards the JSON messages to the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) executor for inclusion in the digital shadow. This constitutes a continuous, one‑way data flow from the physical device to the digital representation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The system uses an MQTT broker as the communication backbone. MQTT provides a lightweight, publish‑subscribe protocol where sensor data are transmitted as JSON (or untyped numeric) messages on hierarchically organised topics, with optional read/write permissions per topic. The broker links the preprocessing component, the AAS executor, and the Thingsboard visualization platform, enabling data exchange between these elements. No further details on bandwidth, latency, redundancy or additional security mechanisms are given in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The development of a digital twin follows the typical MBSE decomposition into abstraction layers: (1) requirements model, (2) functional architecture model, (3) logical architecture model, and (4) product/technical layer model. These are captured using SysML requirement diagrams and internal block diagrams, and refined using methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT. Transition between stages is managed by traceability links defined in the digital‑shadow reference model, which follow the 'originates from' association to propagate data‑trace provenance from processing components to higher‑level models. Lifecycle governance includes a concretization step where a reference model is instantiated into a concrete model by mapping reference concepts to concrete classes and relations.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the twin combines model‑driven development with simulation and data‑calculation models. Architecture description languages (e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs) are used to specify component‑connector structures; behavior may be encoded in state machines, Simulink models, or MontiArc Automaton. Quality assurance is achieved through traceability of data traces to the processing component and the models that generated them, as defined by the ‘originates from’ association in the digital‑shadow reference model. Version control and evolution are handled by the concretization process, where each concrete realization maintains a conformance relation to the reference model, enabling systematic updates of the twin as new processing components or models are added.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The modeling approaches referenced rely on established engineering standards: SysML (OMG), AADL (SAE), ACME (architectural description), MontiArc, and simulation standards such as MATLAB/Simulink. Communication with physical assets can employ the MQTT protocol (implemented by Mosquitto) and IoT platforms such as ThingsBoard, which are open‑source standards for message exchange. No explicit certification or compliance frameworks are mentioned.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].

shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_

of friction characteristics as operating conditions, loads, and
environmental factors change. This continuous data stream allows for the dynamic adaptation of both physics-based (whitebox) and data-driven (black-box) friction models, ensuring
that the models remain accurate and relevant throughout the
robot’s operational life. Such adaptability is crucial for highprecision applications, where even minor deviations in friction
behavior can significantly impact positioning accuracy and
process quality. With digital shadows, it becomes possible
to automatically update model parameters or retrain neural
network-based models as new data becomes available, compensating for wear, temperature changes, or varying payloads.
This results in more robust and reliable friction compensation
strategies, reducing trajectory errors and enhancing the overall
performance of robotic systems. Moreover, the integration of
digital shadows into the robot’s control architecture supports
predictive maintenance and early fault detection, as anomalies
in friction behavior can be detected and addressed proactively.
Ultimately, digital shadows transform friction modeling from
a static, one-time process into a dynamic, continuously improving capability that drives higher accuracy and reliability
in industrial robotics.

Based on this paper, a digital twin of the robot can be
developed to improve machining processes. Here, particular
attention must be paid to the real-time requirements of the calculations and the associated dead time of the control system.
By implementing the friction models in the twin, we can identify the internal joint friction that occurs during runtime, which
can be compensated for by the control system. The resulting
increased accuracy of the robot benefits every application. If,
in addition to the internal friction, compensation of the external



forces caused by the machining process is implemented in
the friction model, simulative experiments can be carried out.
This makes it possible to optimize the machining parameters
and identify critical sub-processes before the start of the
real process. Once a digital twin has been set up with its
communication and data storage components, it serves as a
central data memory. It can therefore be easily supplemented
by other applications, thus accelerating their development
process. In the present application case, for example, the
cutting parameters can be optimized to reduce _CO_ 2 or a
product quality prediction can be developed based on the
production data.

developed to improve machining processes. Here, particular
attention must be paid to the real-time requirements of the calculations and the associated dead time of the control system.
By implementing the friction models in the twin, we can identify the internal joint friction that occurs during runtime, which
can be compensated for by the control system. The resulting
increased accuracy of the robot benefits every application. If,
in addition to the internal friction, compensation of the external



forces caused by the machining process is implemented in
the friction model, simulative experiments can be carried out.
This makes it possible to optimize the machining parameters
and identify critical sub-processes before the start of the
real process. Once a digital twin has been set up with its
communication and data storage components, it serves as a
central data memory. It can therefore be easily supplemented
by other applications, thus accelerating their development
process. In the present application case, for example, the
cutting parameters can be optimized to reduce _CO_ 2 or a
product quality prediction can be developed based on the
production data.


VI. D ISCUSSION AND R ELATED W ORK


_A. Strengths and Limitations of the Approach_

[60]. These approaches are crucial for providing an automated
layer of quality assurance, which is particularly relevant for
the multi-domain modeling in our work.
Furthermore, the integration of real-world data is fundamental for a functioning digital shadow. The use of standardized communication protocols and technologies is essential
for seamless interoperability. Platforms and protocols like
ThingsBoard and MQTT have been identified as key enablers
for connecting IoT devices and sensors to digital shadow
architectures, providing a robust and scalable foundation for
data collection [61]. The use of ThingsBoard for IoT data
storage and visualization reflects current IIoT trends in realtime monitoring and process optimization described in [62].
Our approach utilizes these technologies to ensure a reliable
data flow from the physical robot to its digital counterpart.
The concept of a digital shadow has been successfully
applied across a variety of industrial domains, including
continuous production processes, production planning, and
CNC machine diagnostics [57], [63]–[65]. These applications
demonstrate the versatility and utility of the purpose-driven
paradigm. The field is also moving toward formalizing the
digital shadow concept through reference models and a broader
focus on interoperability [66]. The Asset Administration Shell
(AAS) is a rising standard in this domain, promoting Industry
4.0 interoperability. The integration of SysML structural and



Simulink behavioral models via FMI offers a rigorous multidomain modeling approach [65].


VII. C ONCLUSION


This paper presented a digital shadow for providing higher
accuracy in robot motion control. We applied a systematic,
purpose-driven methodology to build digital shadows, tackling
multi-domain modeling challenges. We have modeled the
robot’s structure as a SysML model and added executable
simulation models to describe the friction in a robot’s first axis.
This information was then integrated into an Asset Administration Shell, together with all relevant data identified when
building and computing the digital shadow. The resulting DS
allows for an integrated linking of the data due to the enhanced
traceability of the included data, not only to the referenced
system element, but also to its method of computation. We
built a pipeline that automatically builds the digital shadow
from an AAS and the robot’s live data, including running
the simulation models and autonomously sending it to a
visualization dashboard.

With this example, we have shown that digital shadows
facilitate the real-time identification of joint friction and allow
for refinement of the used models over time, leading to
continuous friction identification. Our results demonstrate the
feasibility of such data and model integration in dynamic
industrial environments with high-precision applications.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

_C. Digital Shadow Implementation_


In order to make the digital shadow executable, we configured a database according to the data model depicted in Fig. 5,
fed it with data, and displayed it on a dashboard.
We chose the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) as technology to provide all information to build the digital shadow
as the single source of truth. The AAS [46], [47] is a
digital representation of an asset, i.e., physical ones such
as production resources, robots, workpieces, or factories, or
non-physical ones such as models, software, or licenses [48].
AAS contain relevant information throughout the asset life
cycle [49] described in standardized so-called submodels, and
provides interfaces to access the information [50].
Fig. 6 shows the structure of our pipeline. The robot’s
internal sensors send their measurements to a preprocessing
component. It prepares the reference data and sends it to an
MQTT Broker. The broker then converts the data points and
publishes them as JSON. The AAS Executor component
is responsible for interpreting the AAS and executing the
provided information. It receives the values and builds the


**Fig. 6:** Building the digital shadow specified with the AAS.



digital shadow based on the AAS and sends it to Thingsboard
and stores it there. Within Thingsboard, users can visualize
the values.

The AAS contains the concrete DS data model to format the

data streamed to the dashboard and the robot’s interface and

data protocol as MQTT topics to gather the reference data.
Furthermore, the SysML model is included as identification
points for each data trace. The simulation models are included as Functional Mock-up Unit (FMU) [51] exported from
Simulink. FMU is a standardized file format for the exchange
and execution of simulation models. In addition, the AAS
contains specific links between the simulation and the system
model. Each FMU is linked to a SysML block with a simple
mapping. The AAS holds this information hierarchically over
the system’s three sub-components.
As measurement data, we use the input current, the angular
velocity of the motor and the position of the first axis. Before
using the input current signal, a low pass filter must be applied
to remove noise. This is done in the preprocessing step.
The MQTT Broker receives the measurement data. MQTT
is a lightweight, message-based machine-to-machine transport
protocol that utilizes a publish-subscribe architecture [52].
Using topics, it is possible to create channels to which clients
can subscribe to receive messages that are published within
them. Topics are hierarchically organized, and it is often
possible to assign read/write permissions to specific users for
specific topics. Messages are untyped and can contain textual
data such as numbers or JSON. They can also contain binary

[60]. These approaches are crucial for providing an automated
layer of quality assurance, which is particularly relevant for
the multi-domain modeling in our work.
Furthermore, the integration of real-world data is fundamental for a functioning digital shadow. The use of standardized communication protocols and technologies is essential
for seamless interoperability. Platforms and protocols like
ThingsBoard and MQTT have been identified as key enablers
for connecting IoT devices and sensors to digital shadow
architectures, providing a robust and scalable foundation for
data collection [61]. The use of ThingsBoard for IoT data
storage and visualization reflects current IIoT trends in realtime monitoring and process optimization described in [62].
Our approach utilizes these technologies to ensure a reliable
data flow from the physical robot to its digital counterpart.
The concept of a digital shadow has been successfully
applied across a variety of industrial domains, including
continuous production processes, production planning, and
CNC machine diagnostics [57], [63]–[65]. These applications
demonstrate the versatility and utility of the purpose-driven
paradigm. The field is also moving toward formalizing the
digital shadow concept through reference models and a broader
focus on interoperability [66]. The Asset Administration Shell
(AAS) is a rising standard in this domain, promoting Industry
4.0 interoperability. The integration of SysML structural and



Simulink behavioral models via FMI offers a rigorous multidomain modeling approach [65].


VII. C ONCLUSION


This paper presented a digital shadow for providing higher
accuracy in robot motion control. We applied a systematic,
purpose-driven methodology to build digital shadows, tackling
multi-domain modeling challenges. We have modeled the
robot’s structure as a SysML model and added executable
simulation models to describe the friction in a robot’s first axis.
This information was then integrated into an Asset Administration Shell, together with all relevant data identified when
building and computing the digital shadow. The resulting DS
allows for an integrated linking of the data due to the enhanced
traceability of the included data, not only to the referenced
system element, but also to its method of computation. We
built a pipeline that automatically builds the digital shadow
from an AAS and the robot’s live data, including running
the simulation models and autonomously sending it to a
visualization dashboard.

With this example, we have shown that digital shadows
facilitate the real-time identification of joint friction and allow
for refinement of the used models over time, leading to
continuous friction identification. Our results demonstrate the
feasibility of such data and model integration in dynamic
industrial environments with high-precision applications.
